coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots as efficient photosensitizers to enhance visible-light driven photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Omer, Khalid M.
Mohammad, Nian N.
Baban, Shirwan O.
Hassan, Aso Q.
coredata.dc:description: Obtaining photochemically stable, efficient, and earth abundant visible-light photocatalyst is still one of the challenging tasks in the photocatalysis area. In the present paper, a new type of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots, CDs, have been prepared and used as a photosensitizer for a well-known wide-band gap photocatalyst to enhance visible-light driven photocatalytic degradation of an environmental organic pollutant. Blue emitters of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (CDs) have been prepared in an aqueous solution via hydrothermal method. ZnO-CDs nanocomposites were prepared via loading of CDs on ZnO in a low temperature mode, in order to modify the optical, electronic, and surface properties of ZnO nanoparticles. The nanocomposites were characterized by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Raman spectra, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) showed that the nanocomposites of ZnO-CDs enhanced the photodegradation efficiency (95% for 100 min) comparing to the pure ZnO in the UV region (365 nm). Interestingly, ZnO-CDs showed enhanced visible light photodegradation comparing with pure ZnO. Such enhancement is attributed to the increase of charge separation rate, photoluminescence up-conversion properties in CDs, and high absorptivity of the ZnO-CDs nanocomposites. Tailoring various CDs with different chemical reactivities and optical properties will eventually lead to harnessing sufficient portion of the solar spectrum in order to obtain high efficient photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Nanodots
Visible-light photocatalysis
Photosensitizers
Photodegradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Size and morphology of CDs and the nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Scheme for preparation of nitrogen doped CDs. (b) Loading carbon dots to the surface of ZnO.
TEM image shown for (a) CDs alone, (b) ZnO-CDs-hydrothermal.
(a) XRD spectra for CDs, ZnO, and ZnO-CDs. (b) FTIR spectra of CDs and ZnO-CDs nanocomposites.
(a) Absorbance spectra of CDs, ZnO, nanocomposites of CDs (b) Absorbance and emission spectra of CDs in aqueous solution, the PL excited at 400 nm.
Photocatalytic degradation of 10 mg/L of methylene blue in aqueous solution using ZnO-CDs nanocomposite. (a) Irradiation under Visible light, 20 W (b) Irradiation under UV light, 365 nm, 15 W.
Photocatalyst stability under visible light for five successive cycles. Pure CDs are black lines, Pure ZnO are blue lines, ZnO-CDs are red lines.

Scheme of the electron transfer and shuttle from ZnO to CDs. (a) Under visible light irradiation, LED white light, 20 W. λ1 is bigger than λ2; λ2 is the emitted light from CDs luminescence which might promote electrons of ZnO from VB to CB. (b) Under UV light, 365 nm.15 W.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots as efficient photosensitizers to enhance visible-light driven photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Qliasan Street, Sulaimani City, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Education, University of Salahadin, Kirkuk Street, Erbil, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Qliasan Street, Sulaimani City, Kurdistan Region, Iraq.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Obtaining photochemically stable, efficient, and earth abundant visible-light photocatalyst is still one of the challenging tasks in the photocatalysis area. In the present paper, a new type of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots, CDs, have been prepared and used as a photosensitizer for a well-known wide-band gap photocatalyst to enhance visible-light driven photocatalytic degradation of an environmental organic pollutant. Blue emitters of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (CDs) have been prepared in an aqueous solution via hydrothermal method. ZnO-CDs nanocomposites were prepared via loading of CDs on ZnO in a low temperature mode, in order to modify the optical, electronic, and surface properties of ZnO nanoparticles. The nanocomposites were characterized by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Raman spectra, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) showed that the nanocomposites of ZnO-CDs enhanced the photodegradation efficiency (95% for 100 min) comparing to the pure ZnO in the UV region (365 nm). Interestingly, ZnO-CDs showed enhanced visible light photodegradation comparing with pure ZnO. Such enhancement is attributed to the increase of charge separation rate, photoluminescence up-conversion properties in CDs, and high absorptivity of the ZnO-CDs nanocomposites. Tailoring various CDs with different chemical reactivities and optical properties will eventually lead to harnessing sufficient portion of the solar spectrum in order to obtain high efficient photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis, as a green approach, offers great potential for removal of environmental pollutants and conversion of solar to fuel. In the catalysis process, photocatalyst is considered as an important key to achieve efficient degradation process with low cost and environmental friendly material [1,2]. Thus, obtaining the earth abundant low cost, photostable, and high efficient catalyst is the ultimate goal in successful photocatalysis.

Recently, carbon nanodots (CDs), which encompass of carbon quantum dots and graphene quantum dots, are becoming one of the promising nanomaterials due to their unique optical and catalytic properties. They are few nanometers in size, easy to fabricate and have high luminescence properties, which will make CDs be implemented in many applications, such as; bio-sensors, bio-imaging, and light emitting diodes [3–5].

Using CDs in catalysis still is in its early stage, and much works have not done yet. One of the obstacles that hinders applicability and through characterization of CDs in both electrocatalysis and photocatalysis is the intrinsic heterogeneity nature of the dots [6–8].

However, some handful works have been done on using the CDs for photocatalytic degradation of organic compounds [9–12]. Facile and green synthesis with the high catalytic potential of carbon dots will make this type of carbon nanomaterials strong alternative for degradation and environmental remediation [13].

Hybridization of carbon nanomaterials with inorganic nanomaterials exhibits a successful strategy for developing new functionalities and improving their properties [3,14–17]. Thus, methodologies for constructing organic nanomaterial with inorganic materials integrated in well-defined architectures are of significant importance and may pave a way for screening new efficient photocatalytic materials.

ZnO nanoparticles are among the semiconductor nanomaterials that have been used extensively for photocatalytic degradation due to its good photocatalytic activity, low cost, abundance of its components and ease of preparation in bulk or nanostructured forms. However, ZnO high relatively large band gap and absorb very small portion of the solar spectrum, which limits its use for visible light degradation. Still, much should be done in order to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency [18,19].

Herein, we present a facile method for preparation of nitrogen doped blue emitter of nitrogen doped CDs and loading onto ZnO nanoparticles without using high temperature. Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) using pure ZnO and ZnO sensitized CDs were examined under both UV and visible light. Fig. 1 shows the preparation scheme of CDs and ZnO-CDs.

All chemicals used were on analytical grade. Uric acid (UA), glucose, zinc acetate, and chloroform were bought from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Chloroform with ACS grade were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Sigma Aldrich, 99%, USA). Dialysis membrane, Spectra/Por 3 Dialysis Tubing, 1000 MWCO was purchased from Thomas Scientific (Thomas Scientific, Swedesboro, NJ. USA)

The morphology and size of the CDs and nanocomposites were investigated using transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopic TEM; JEOL, Japan) at 200 KV. The samples for TEM were prepared by casting a drop of dilute solution of CDs, and nanocomposites onto a carbon- coated copper grid. FTIR spectra were obtained on Cary 660 ATR-FTIR connected to ATR (Agilent Technologies, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on Krato Axis Supra photoelectron spectrometer (kratos, UK). Some drops of CDs were port on 1 cm *1 cm of a gold coated glass slide, then left overnight under argon gas before transferring to XPS chamber. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Cary 60 Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained on Empyrean X-ray diffractometer, (PANalytical, Netherland). UV lamp source 365 nm, 15 W. Visible White light 20 W (Vitone, 1000 K). Fluorescence measurements (PL) were recorded on Cary Eclipsed Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA).

Uric acid and glucose, 2 g of each, were mixed and dissolved in 15 mL water. Two to three drops of concentrated HCl were added, then the solution transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave for 21 h at 180 °C. The resulting solution was then filtered and the filtrate portion centrifuged for 20 min at 12,000 rpm to get rid-off big particles. The nanodots in the aqueous solution were then further purified with double-step purification, first extraction into chloroform and then dialysis using 1000 MWCO for 2 h [20].

Nanoparticles of ZnO has been prepared as reported in literature. In brief, A stock solutions of Zn(CH3COO)2.2H2O (0.1 M) was prepared in 50 mL methanol under stirring for an hour. To this stock solution 25 mL of NaOH (0.2 M) solution prepared in methanol was added under continuous stirring in order to get the pH of the solution 8. The solution was transferred into Teflon-lined sealed stainless-steel autoclaves and maintained at 200 °C for 6 h under autogenous pressure. It was then allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The resulting white solid products were washed with methanol, filtered and then dried in air in a laboratory oven at 60 °C.

ZnO nanoparticles (5 mg) were mixed with 2 mL of CDs (1 mg/mL) and the solution was stirred for one hour. The collected solution can be used for photocatalytic experiments. The resulting solution produce no blue emission of carbon dots, assigning that most CDs were loaded on ZnO surface. Figure S1 shows photos of CDS under UV irradiation and ZnO-CDs powder.

Photocatalytic activity was determined by adding the CDs solution or nanocomposites of ZnO-CDs (20 mL, 1 mg/L) to 50 mL of MB (10 mg/L). The solution was irradiated under UV (10 W high-pressure Hg lamp) at ambient atmosphere, and under white LED lamp as a source of visible light (LED, 3000 K, 20 W). The concentration of the residual molecule of MB was determined by measuring of the absorbance at 640 nm. The solution of MB dye and ZnO-CDs photocatalyst was stirred in the dark for 30 min prior to illumination to exclude the effect of dye adsorption on the photocatalytic activity. Each photocatalytic measurement was performed three times to obtain the desired reproducibility.

CDs were synthesized in aqueous solution in the pH range from 2 to 4 using hydrothermal method. The CDs were purified using double-step purification method which was developed in our lab [20]. The double-step purification is polarity-based purification followed by size-based purification. In the polarity-based purification which is simple solvent extraction, the same functional groups nanodots were extracted from the aqueous layer to the chloroform layer. In the size-based step which is dialysis membrane, the same size nanodots were obtained using dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO). It has been shown that using double step purification could produce CDs with more uniform nanoparticles in size and functional groups [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size and morphology of CDs and the nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was used in order to measure the size of the CDs nanoparticles, and as shown in Fig. 2 a. The CDs were monodisperse around ∼ 2–5 nm. Same conclusion obtained using AFM (Figure S2). As shown in Fig. 2b the TEM image of ZnO-CDs nanocomposites, that sizes are around 40 nm. Because size of CDs is about ten time smaller than the size of ZnO, most likely the CDs are hard to be seen on the surface of ZnO

X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded for all CDs, ZnO, and ZnO-CDs, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The ZnO spectra show the characteristic diffraction peaks of ZnO which corresponds to typical Wurtzite ZnO phase. The XRD pattern of CDs shows broad peaks around 26°, corresponding to the amorphous structure of graphite in CDs (Fig. 3) [22,23]. The XRD pattern for ZnO-CDs shows both peaks; CDs and ZnO diffractions peaks, assigning for loading of CDs on the surface of ZnO nanoparticles.

FTIR spectra were recorded in order to characterize the surface structure of the nanocomposites, as shown in the Fig. 3b. The broad absorption band from 2600 to 3500 cm−1 is corresponded to OH, C–H stretching of the functional groups on CDs surface. Absorption peaks at 1593 cm−1 and 1405 cm-1 are related to OCO asymmetric and symmetric vibrations.

The clear absorption bands at 400–600 cm−1 in ZnO-CDs are attributed to the stretching vibrations of Zn–O [24]. Aromatic CNH at 1271 cm−1, and COH at 1124 cm−1 were observed (Fig. 3b), assigning for NH and OH functional groups on the surface of CDs [25].

In order to further investigate the surface composition and doping of nitrogen in the CDs, high resolution XPS spectra were carried out (Fig. 4 ). The XPS survey spectra are shown in Fig. 4a displays three characteristic peaks: O 1 s (531.3–532.5 eV), N 1 s (400.3–402.3 eV), C1 s (284.2–288.5 eV), and N 1 s (400 eV). Each peak can be further analyzed and fitted as shown in Fig. 4.
O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4b) Shows three peaks centered at 531.3 eV, 532.5 eV, and 533.5 eV corresponding to C = O, C–O, and O-H respectively [26].
C 1 s Spectrum (Fig. 4c) shows four characteristic peaks at 284.2 eV, 286.1 eV, 287.2 eV, and 288.5 eV corresponding to graphitic sp2 C = C, C–O/C−OH, C–N, and C = O respectively [27,28].
The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4d) shows two resolved peaks, the lower energy peak at 400.3 eV corresponds to C-N-C nitrogen atom and 402.1 eV correspond to N-H eV [29,30].
Based on XPS results, one can confirm the CDs are nitrogen-doped carbon materials.

The absorption and emission spectra of CDs in the aqueous solution were recorded as shown in Fig. 5 . CDs exhibits two absorption peaks at 240 nm, which assigned for π to π* transition, and another peak around 365 nm that can be attributed to n to π* transition [31]. The photoluminescence spectra of CDs centered around 425 nm when excited at 365 nm. The emission spectra are shown in the Fig. 5a, and it centered around 425 nm.
Absorption spectra of CDs, ZnO nanoparticles, and ZnO-CDs nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 4.
The absorption spectrum of ZnO nanoparticles shows the characteristic spectrum with its fundamental absorption edge rising at 381 nm.
The ZnO/CQDs displays characteristic absorption bands at 365 nm due to n−π* electronic transition of the CQDs and a weak absorption edge at 395 nm of ZnO.

Methylene blue, (10 mg/L) was chosen as an organic dye for performing the photocatalytic degradation. Fig. 6 shows photocatalytic results in both UV and visible light irradiation for pure CDs, pure ZnO and ZnO-CDs nanocomposites. Visible light irradiation of pure CDs showed 50% degradation of MB at 25 h. While irradiation of pure ZnO under visible light showed 60% degradation of the original MB. However, ZnO-CDs nanocomposite showed the synergistic effect and could degrade 90% of the original MB. Here one can notice the effect of photosensitization of CDs on the catalytic efficiency.

Under 365 nm of UV light, ZnO-CDs showed improved photodegradation comparing to bare ZnO or CD. Degradation efficiency as 95% was achieved using ZnO-CDs in 90 min, and in 100 min using ZnO-CDs. Nanocomposites showed enhancement over pure ZnO, which photodegrade 95% in 140 min.

The stability of nanocomposites was tested for different successive cycle, after washing with ethanol and drying the nanocomposite in oven for 24 h under 60 °C. Fig. 7 shows visible light photocatalysis stability for five-time runs which indicates highly stable photocatalyst.
The possible mechanism for enhancement in both UV and visible light can be attributed to some reasons:

The first reason of the enhancement both in UV and visible range is attributed to increase the charge separation process, inhibition of the recombination of electron-hole as shown in Fig. 6a and b. Higher absorption coefficient of the nanocomposites, as shown in the optical spectra, could be a reason for improving catalytic activity [32].

The efficient charge separation can increase the life time of the charge carrier and inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs which will enhance the efficiency of the interfacial charge transfer to adsorbed substrates, and thus increase the number of carriers adsorbed by contaminant [33].

Another reason for visible-light photocatalytic enhancement is nitrogen doped CDs possess up-conversion photoluminescence properties (Fig. 8 a), which they can absorb longer wavelength (visible light, λ1) and then emit shorter wavelength through up-conversion (λ2), which in turn can excite ZnO to form electron-hole pair (Fig. 8b) [34,35].

The third reason is loading CDs with ZnO lead to increase the interaction of ZnO with the visible light of the solar spectrum, due to coupling of conduction band of ZnO with pi states of graphitic CDs [14]. The electronic coupling between ZnO and CDs would lead to red shift of band gap of nanocomposite comparing to pure ZnO.

Photostable nanocomposites photocatalyst were prepared from new carbon nanodots and ZnO nanoparticles. CDs serves as photosensitizer to the wide band gap ZnO nanoparticles. The nanocomposites showed catalytic activity under UV and visible light for degradation of an organic compound in aqueous solution. Interestingly, the nanocomposites showed enhanced photocatalytic efficiency comparing to pure ZnO using both UV and visible light sources. Almost 90% of the methylene blue was removed under irradiation with a weak visible light (20 W). We showed that CDs play a critical role in the catalytic enhancement, which opens a door for further tailoring of CDs to match the desired enhancement using more portion of the solar spectrum with higher photocatalytic efficiency.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Nian M. Mohammad thanks Ministry of Higher Education in KRG-Iraq for Funding the PhD program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2018.05.041.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
